_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-4 General Crush Injury (Revised 11/17/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-4: General Crush Injury — MDHHS Approved 11/17/25, Reviewed 2025
purpose: Consider when patient has been entrapped at the scene for more than one hour, with one or more full extremities trapped by an object capable of causing a crush injury (machinery, dirt, rock, rubble). Crush Syndrome should be suspected with entrapment/compression > 1 hour, especially involving a large muscle mass/group. Treatment should begin BEFORE patient is removed when practical.

sections.title: Crush Injury Treatment
points: Follow General Trauma-Treatment Protocol; identify and treat life threats
Pediatric patients (< 14 years of age): follow pediatric dosing below; albuterol use for crush injury does not appear on MI MEDIC
Assess for signs of Compartment Syndrome or Crush Syndrome
Use tourniquet as indicated per Tourniquet Application-Procedure Protocol
Administer oxygen to patient if environment allows

Beginning immediately PRIOR TO release from entrapment and continued until AFTER extrication: administer albuterol by continuous nebulization (per MCA selection — may be EMT skill); Adults and Pediatrics: albuterol may be continued to maximum dose of 20 mg (eight 2.5 mg/3 mL nebules)

Establish large bore IV(s) and/or IO per Vascular Access and IV Fluid Therapy-Procedure Protocol and administer fluid bolus PRIOR TO removal of patient when practical (Specialist skill); Adults: 1 liter IV/IO wide open followed by 500–1,000 mL/hr; Pediatrics: 20 mL/kg IV/IO wide open followed by 10 mL/kg/hr
Treat patient pain per Pain Management-Procedure Protocol
Initiate cardiac monitoring and assess for hyperkalemia (wide QRS or peaked T waves) — monitor continuously for changes (Specialist skill)

If extrication is prolonged, hyperkalemia is suspected (peaked T waves, widened QRS, hypotension), or patient proceeds to cardiac arrest: administer calcium chloride (Specialist skill); Adults: 1 gm slow IVP over 5 minutes; Pediatrics: 20 mg/kg slow IVP over 5 minutes, max dose 1 gram over 5 minutes
Perform repeated 12-Lead ECG if conditions allow (per MCA selection — may be BLS or Specialist procedure) per 12 Lead ECG-Procedure Protocol

mca_selections_required.description: MCA must indicate whether EMT may administer nebulized albuterol for crush injury
May be designated as a BLS skill or Specialist (AEMT/Paramedic) skill

key_drugs.name: Albuterol (Continuous Nebulization — Crush)
key_drugs.dose: Continuous nebulization — begin before release from entrapment; maximum 20 mg (eight 2.5 mg/3 mL nebules); same dose adult and pediatric
key_drugs.indication: Crush injury — adjunctive treatment to shift potassium intracellularly and reduce hyperkalemia risk during extrication
key_drugs.scope: Per MCA selection — EMT option available
key_drugs.notes: NOTE: Does not appear on MI MEDIC. Begin immediately prior to release and continue through extrication.

key_drugs.name: LR or NS IV/IO Fluid Bolus
key_drugs.dose: Adults: 1 liter wide open then 500–1,000 mL/hr; Pediatrics: 20 mL/kg wide open then 10 mL/kg/hr
key_drugs.indication: Crush injury fluid resuscitation — start prior to release from entrapment when practical
key_drugs.notes: Pre-emptive fluid loading before release helps prevent reperfusion injury.

key_drugs.name: Calcium Chloride
key_drugs.dose: Adults: 1 gm slow IVP over 5 minutes; Pediatrics: 20 mg/kg slow IVP over 5 minutes (max 1 gram over 5 minutes)
key_drugs.indication: Prolonged extrication; suspected hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, widened QRS, hypotension); cardiac arrest from crush
key_drugs.side_effects: Bradycardia if administered rapidly; tissue necrosis if extravasation
key_drugs.notes: Administer slowly over 5 minutes. Monitor ECG during administration.

deterioration_flags: Peaked T waves or widened QRS on monitor — suspect hyperkalemia; administer calcium chloride (Specialist skill) prior to release
Cardiac arrest during extrication — consider hyperkalemia as etiology; calcium chloride indicated
Hypotension after release — aggressive fluid resuscitation; contact Medical Control
Compartment syndrome signs (pain out of proportion, paresthesia, pallor, pulselessness, paralysis) — expedite transport
scope_notes: Scene safety, tourniquet application, oxygen: all levels
Albuterol nebulized: per MCA selection — EMT option available
IV/IO access and fluid resuscitation: Specialist skill
Cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG: Specialist skill; 12-lead may be BLS per MCA selection
Pain management: refer to Pain Management-Procedure Protocol
